summary: The National Labor Relations Board issued a ruling that redefines 'employee' to include franchise and contractor workers, allowing them to negotiate directly with parent corporations like McDonald's rather than just local franchise owners. Previously, McDonald's maintained legal separation from its workers despite controlling nearly every aspect of their jobs through surveillance, policies, and tight franchise margins. This framework prevented large-scale unionization since workers could only bargain with individual franchise owners operating on thin margins. The new 'joint employer' designation means parent corporations now share liability for worker treatment and cannot simply shut down franchises that unionize.
key_points: 90% of McDonald's low-level employees technically work for franchise owners, not the corporation, despite the corporation controlling their work conditions at a minute level
The previous legal framework prevented effective unionization by forcing workers to negotiate only with margin-constrained local franchise owners
The 'joint employer' ruling extends beyond franchises to contractors, making it harder for corporations to create legal distance from workers they effectively control
Parent corporations can no longer shut down a franchise simply for unionizing under the new ruling
money_quotes: 90% of the low-level employees at McDonalds don't technically work for McDonalds at all; instead, they work for a local franchise owner, who in turn rents stuff, including the McDonalds brand, from the parent corporation.

In spite of the technical and legal framework, the parent corporation controlled the burger flippers at a minute level, through surveillance, customer service policy, branding requirements, and most importantly through controlling the margins of the franchise owner.

The legal separation, which was solidly working until yesterday, meant that employees couldn't complain about bad treatment of their employer, McDonalds, but rather had to complain only about the way the local franchise owner treated them.
It will likely mean that it will in general be much harder for corporations to create legal distance between itself and the people hired to do work for that company.
key_insight: Corporations that control workers through surveillance, policy, and economic pressure cannot escape liability simply by inserting a legal intermediary between themselves and those workers.
